children.name: Alligator mississippiensis
children.description: Once threatened with extinction; recovered to over 5 million; keystone species creating gator holes — depressions that retain water through dry season and sustain entire wetland communities; an American conservation success story.

children.name: Alligator sinensis
children.description: Critically endangered; fewer than 120 wild individuals; confined to lower Yangtze River; heavily farmed.

children.name: Caiman crocodilus
children.description: Most abundant crocodilian; bony bridge between eyes gives spectacled appearance; millions harvested for leather; ecological keystone of Amazonian floodplains.

children.name: Caiman yacare
children.description: Dominant caiman of the Pantanal; estimated 10 million individuals; main prey of giant otters and jaguars.

children.name: Caiman latirostris
children.description: Distinguished by its unusually broad, shovel-shaped snout adapted for crushing hard-shelled prey such as snails, freshwater mussels, and crustaceans. Found in eastern South America from Brazil south to Argentina, favouring marshes, swamps, and mangrove fringes rather than open rivers. Severely depleted by the leather trade in the 20th century, populations have partially recovered thanks to conservation management and egg harvesting programs in Argentina. Females build large mound nests of vegetation and guard their clutches attentively.

children.name: Melanosuchus niger
children.description: Largest predator in the Amazon basin at up to 5 m; hunts capybara, fish, and deer; nearly extinct by 1970s due to hunting; now recovering.

children.name: Paleosuchus trigonatus
children.description: Most terrestrial crocodilian; often found far from water in forest; bony armour protects from large predators.

children.name: Paleosuchus palpebrosus
children.description: The smallest living crocodilian, rarely exceeding 1.5 metres in length, yet armoured with exceptionally hard, ossified skin that offers protection against large cats and anacondas. Found throughout the Amazon and Orinoco basins, preferring fast-flowing, rocky streams in dense rainforest — habitats too cool and swift for most other crocodilians. Despite its small size it is a capable predator of fish, amphibians, and small mammals. Named after the French naturalist Georges Cuvier. Populations are stable and it is classified as Least Concern.
